Near-twice-weekly operations by late 2023 imply the threshold was within the demonstrated run-rate, while any multi-week grounding, range bottleneck, or pad constraint could erase the small margin. I put the probability well above even because the threshold is close to the 2023 achieved level and below recent growth trend, but not near certain because launch systems have lumpy operational risk and 100 requires very few extended disruptions.
attempts.strongest_yes: The threshold required only modest growth from 96 launches in 2023 to 100 in 2024, after SpaceX had grown from 31 to 61 to 96 over the prior two years and was already operating near twice-weekly cadence by late 2023.
attempts.strongest_no: At 96 launches, SpaceX had little slack: a Falcon 9 grounding, range or weather bottlenecks, pad downtime, customer delays, or management/engineering attention shifting toward Starship could easily cut several launches and leave the total below 100.

cases.question: As of January 1, 2024, will the United Kingdom hold a general election in calendar year 2024?

output.rationale: Averaged 3 differentiated forecaster probabilities. Review component attempts for disagreement and source quality.
output.cited_sources: [{"title":"Fixed evidence packet","claim":"The UK Parliament elected in December 2019 had to face another general election by January 2025, Sunak had discretion over timing, and a 2024 election was broadly expected while a non-2024 outcome required using the latest possible timetable."},{"claim":"Fixed evidence packet states that the Parliament elected in December 2019 had to face another general election by January 2025, that Sunak had discretion over timing, that Conservatives trailed Labour heavily in late-2023 polling, and that a 2024 election was broadly expected unless the latest possible timetable was used."},{"title":"Fixed evidence packet","claim":"The UK Parliament elected in December 2019 had to face another general election by January 2025 under maximum term rules."},{"title":"Fixed evidence packet","claim":"Prime Minister Rishi Sunak had discretion over election timing within the legal window."},{"title":"Fixed evidence packet","claim":"A 2024 election was broadly expected because waiting until January 2025 would leave little flexibility and could be politically risky."}]

attempts.rationale: Base-rate starting point: a UK Parliament elected in December 2019 was near the end of its maximum term by January 1, 2024, with the legal deadline forcing an election by January 2025. In a one-year forecast window covering almost all remaining politically usable time, the historical/reference-class expectation is high: governments usually avoid leaving elections to the absolute last possible date because it removes flexibility and exposes them to shocks. Case-specific evidence pushes further toward yes: the evidence packet says a 2024 election was broadly expected, with spring, autumn, or very late 2024 all plausible. The main reason not to go too high is that the prime minister had discretion and a badly trailing governing party might rationally delay in hope of improved conditions. Still, a non-2024 result required using the latest possible timetable, so I assign a high but not certain probability.
attempts.strongest_yes: The Parliament had to face an election by January 2025, and a 2024 election was broadly expected because waiting until the final timetable would be politically risky and leave little room for adjustment.
attempts.strongest_no: A prime minister whose party was trailing badly could choose to delay as long as legally possible, hoping for polling recovery, economic improvement, opposition mistakes, or a more favorable campaign environment.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether Sunak prioritized retaining timing flexibility or maximizing time for political recovery","Whether late-2023 Labour polling leads persisted or narrowed enough to alter the timing calculus","Whether operational, legislative, economic, or party-management constraints made a January 2025 timetable attractive despite the risks"]
attempts.premortem: If this forecast resolves false, the likely explanation is that the government judged its position so weak that delay had positive option value, accepted the risks of the latest possible timetable, and pushed polling day into January 2025 rather than choosing any 2024 slot.
attempts.wildcards: ["Major crisis or economic shock changes the perceived best timing","Leadership instability or internal Conservative Party conflict affects election timing","Unexpected polling movement creates incentive for either an earlier or later election"]
attempts.cited_sources: [{"title":"Fixed evidence packet","claim":"The UK Parliament elected in December 2019 had to face another general election by January 2025, Sunak had discretion over timing, and a 2024 election was broadly expected while a non-2024 outcome required using the latest possible timetable."}]

attempts.rationale: The concrete timeline strongly favors a 2024 polling day. The Parliament elected in December 2019 must face a general election by January 2025, so the practical choice set is mostly spring 2024, autumn 2024, very late 2024, or the latest possible January 2025 timetable. Because the governing party is far behind in polling, the prime minister has an incentive to preserve option value and choose a moment in 2024 if conditions improve even modestly, rather than voluntarily compressing the timetable into January 2025. Waiting until the final legal window creates operational and political risk with little flexibility. The main reason not to forecast near certainty is that a badly trailing government may delay as long as legally possible in hope of an improvement, and that latest-possible strategy could push polling day into January 2025.
attempts.strongest_yes: A 2024 election is the default practical path: the legal deadline is January 2025, possible 2024 windows are numerous, and using the very latest timetable is a narrow strategy with high political and logistical risk.
attempts.strongest_no: A government far behind in polling may rationally wait as long as possible, hoping economic or political conditions improve, and prime ministerial discretion makes a January 2025 polling day legally possible if the latest timetable is used.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether Sunak prioritizes preserving flexibility or delaying to the absolute legal limit.","Whether polling, economic conditions, or internal Conservative Party pressure improve enough to trigger a 2024 election date.","Whether strategic preference converges on autumn/late-2024 versus the latest possible January 2025 timetable.","Whether unexpected governing crises force an earlier election or, conversely, make delay more attractive."]
attempts.premortem: This forecast resolves false if the government, facing consistently poor polling, decides there is no favorable 2024 window and deliberately uses the latest possible timetable, pushing polling day into January 2025 despite the flexibility and optics costs.
attempts.wildcards: ["A sudden improvement in Conservative polling could prompt a snap spring or autumn 2024 election.","A severe crisis, leadership instability, or parliamentary breakdown could force an earlier 2024 election.","A short-term economic rebound or policy success could create a preferred election window in 2024.","Persistent bad polling with no recovery could make maximal delay to January 2025 the least bad option."]
attempts.cited_sources: [{"claim":"Fixed evidence packet states that the Parliament elected in December 2019 had to face another general election by January 2025, that Sunak had discretion over timing, that Conservatives trailed Labour heavily in late-2023 polling, and that a 2024 election was broadly expected unless the latest possible timetable was used."}]

attempts.rationale: The base case is that the UK holds a general election during calendar year 2024 because the 2019 Parliament was near the end of its maximum term, the election had to occur by January 2025, and the evidence packet says a 2024 election was broadly expected. From first principles, a prime minister with timing discretion usually wants some flexibility over campaign conditions, and leaving the election to the latest possible timetable creates operational and political risk. The main reason not to go too high is that the resolution requires polling day itself to fall in 2024, not merely dissolution or campaign launch, and the prime minister legally could attempt the latest possible schedule into January 2025 if he judged delay less bad than facing voters in 2024.
attempts.strongest_yes: A 2024 polling day is the natural consequence of the legal deadline: the Parliament elected in December 2019 had to face another general election by January 2025, and the fixed evidence says spring, autumn, or very late 2024 were all plausible expected windows. Waiting until January 2025 would use the latest possible timetable and leave little room for contingencies.
attempts.strongest_no: The prime minister had discretion within the legal window, and the governing Conservatives were trailing badly. If all available 2024 windows looked politically hopeless, Sunak could rationally delay to the latest possible timetable, hoping for better economic news, opposition mistakes, or a narrower polling gap, producing a January 2025 polling day and resolving false.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether Sunak would prioritize retaining optionality or maximizing delay in hopes conditions improved.","Whether legal and procedural timetable details made a January 2025 polling day politically or practically acceptable.","Whether late-2023 polling deficits made an early or mid-2024 election unattractive enough to force delay.","Whether an external shock in 2024 would encourage postponement to the latest legal date."]

attempts.wildcards: ["Major economic improvement or deterioration changing the preferred timing.","Leadership instability inside the Conservative Party altering election timing incentives.","A national crisis or severe administrative disruption making a late-2024 election less attractive.","A tactical choice to hold dissolution in 2024 but polling day in January 2025, which would not satisfy the stated resolution criteria."]

cases.question: As of January 1, 2025, will Apple release a foldable iPhone before January 1, 2026?

output.rationale: Averaged 3 differentiated forecaster probabilities. Review component attempts for disagreement and source quality.
output.cited_sources: [{"title":"Fixed evidence packet","claim":"By early 2025 Apple had not announced a foldable iPhone; rumors and analyst notes pointed to 2026 or later as a more plausible first launch window; Apple tends to wait for mature hardware categories and prioritize durability, crease quality, software polish, and margins."},{"claim":"Fixed evidence packet states that by early 2025 Apple had not announced a foldable iPhone, rumors and analyst notes pointed to 2026 or later as more plausible, and a pre-2026 release would require announcement and sale during the 2025 product cycle despite no firm public launch signal."}]

attempts.rationale: Starting from base rates, major iPhone form-factor changes are infrequent and usually have visible supply-chain, analyst, developer, and product-cycle signals well before launch. Foldable smartphones had already been commercial for years by early 2025, but Apple had not entered the category, suggesting a low annual hazard rate despite the category's existence. In a reference class of Apple adopting mature hardware categories, Apple often waits until durability, software integration, manufacturing yield, and margins meet its thresholds. The fixed evidence says early-2025 rumors pointed more often to 2026 or later, and that there was no firm public launch signal at the start of the year. A release before 2026 would require Apple to announce and sell a foldable iPhone within the 2025 cycle from a weak starting signal, so the base-rate and case-specific evidence both point to a low probability, though not zero because Apple can keep products secret and launch quickly once supply is ready.
attempts.strongest_yes: Foldable phones were already a proven commercial category, Apple was reportedly exploring displays and hinges, and Apple could choose to enter once it believed durability, crease quality, software polish, and margins were acceptable; a surprise 2025 launch is possible if internal work was more advanced than public rumors indicated.
attempts.strongest_no: As of January 1, 2025 Apple had not announced a foldable iPhone, public rumors and analyst notes favored 2026 or later, and Apple's historical pattern is to wait for mature hardware and avoid launches where durability, display quality, software polish, or margins are not yet compelling.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether Apple's internal foldable iPhone program was materially more advanced than public evidence suggested by early 2025.","Whether suppliers had quietly solved yield, hinge durability, crease, and display-cost constraints in time for a 2025 product cycle.","Whether Apple would classify and market a foldable-display phone as an iPhone rather than a different product category.","Whether competitive pressure from Samsung and Chinese vendors could force an earlier-than-planned launch."]

attempts.wildcards: ["A strategically important anniversary or product-line reset leading Apple to accept lower early margins.","A major supplier breakthrough in foldable OLED durability or crease reduction early enough for mass production.","A competitor's unexpectedly successful foldable flagship pressuring Apple to accelerate launch timing.","Apple releasing a limited-volume premium foldable iPhone to test the category before a broader rollout."]

attempts.rationale: As of 2025-01-01, the inside-view case for a foldable iPhone release before 2026-01-01 is weak. The resolution requires not just development activity but a public release of an iPhone with a foldable display during the 2025 product cycle. The fixed evidence says Apple had not announced such a device by early 2025, had repeatedly delayed or avoided the category, and that rumors and analyst notes more often pointed to 2026 or later. Mechanistically, a 2025 release would likely require supply-chain readiness, final hardware validation, software adaptation, margin confidence, and a near-term launch signal already visible by the start of 2025. The main reason probability is not zero is that Apple can keep products secret and could enter an established category abruptly if internal readiness were higher than public signals implied. Still, given no firm launch signal and multiple blockers, a 2025 public release looks unlikely.

If Apple had been doing quiet exploratory work on displays and hinges for years, it could theoretically convert that work into a 2025 release while maintaining secrecy until announcement.

A true resolution requires announcement and sale before 2026-01-01, which is a high bar for a new iPhone form factor with durability, crease, software, and margin risks.

cases.question: As of January 1, 2024, will the US Federal Reserve cut the federal funds target range at least once in calendar year 2024?

output.rationale: Averaged 3 differentiated forecaster probabilities. Review component attempts for disagreement and source quality.
output.cited_sources: [{"title":"Fixed evidence packet","claim":"The federal funds target range was 5.25% to 5.50%, inflation had declined but remained above target, December 2023 FOMC projections implied multiple 2024 cuts if inflation kept cooling, labor markets were resilient, and Fed/market communications had shifted toward cuts as a plausible base case."},{"claim":"Fixed evidence packet states the federal funds target range was 5.25% to 5.50% after the 2022-2023 hiking cycle."},{"claim":"Fixed evidence packet states inflation had declined substantially from its 2022 peak but remained above the 2% target."},{"claim":"Fixed evidence packet states December 2023 FOMC projections implied multiple rate cuts during 2024 if inflation kept cooling."},{"claim":"Fixed evidence packet states labor markets were still resilient, creating uncertainty about timing."},{"claim":"Fixed evidence packet states market pricing and Fed communications had shifted toward cuts as a plausible base case."}]

attempts.rationale: Starting from base rates, a policy rate at the end of an aggressive hiking cycle is a high-risk state for subsequent easing within the next calendar year, especially when inflation has already declined materially and official projections imply cuts. The question asks whether there is at least one cut at any point in 2024, not how many cuts or how early they occur, so the event has a broad path to resolving true. The fixed packet says the target range was restrictive at 5.25% to 5.50%, inflation had cooled substantially though remained above target, and December 2023 FOMC projections implied multiple 2024 cuts if cooling continued. That makes at least one cut the base case. I discount from very high confidence because resilient labor markets and above-target inflation could lead the FOMC to hold rates through 2024 if inflation reaccelerated or progress stalled.
attempts.strongest_yes: The combination of a completed aggressive hiking cycle, substantially lower inflation, restrictive policy, and FOMC projections implying multiple 2024 cuts makes at least one cut during the year more likely than not. Because only one cut is needed, the forecast can survive delayed timing.
attempts.strongest_no: Inflation was still above the 2% target and labor markets were resilient, so the FOMC could decide that cutting would risk reigniting inflation. A reacceleration or stall in disinflation could plausibly keep the target range unchanged all year.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether inflation continues cooling enough for the FOMC to gain confidence before year-end 2024","Whether labor-market resilience keeps demand and wage pressure too strong for cuts","Whether financial conditions loosen prematurely and make the Fed more cautious","Whether the FOMC prioritizes inflation credibility over easing into slower growth"]
attempts.premortem: This forecast resolves false if inflation stops improving or reaccelerates early in 2024, labor markets remain strong, and the FOMC concludes that any cut would be premature. In that world, December 2023 projections prove conditional rather than predictive, and the Fed holds the 5.25% to 5.50% range all year to preserve inflation-fighting credibility.
attempts.wildcards: ["A sudden recession or financial-stability shock could force earlier or larger cuts","An energy-price or supply shock could push inflation higher and block cuts","A sharp labor-market deterioration could make at least one cut nearly certain","Unexpectedly loose financial conditions could delay easing despite cooling inflation"]

attempts.rationale: As of 2024-01-01, the target range is already restrictive at 5.25% to 5.50% after a rapid hiking cycle, inflation has cooled materially from its peak, and the December 2023 FOMC projections indicate that policymakers themselves expect multiple cuts in 2024 conditional on continued disinflation. The question only requires at least one cut by 2024-12-31, not early or multiple cuts, so the bar is relatively low. Even if resilient labor markets delay action, the Fed has a full calendar year for inflation progress, labor softening, or risk-management concerns to justify one reduction. The main blocker is a renewed inflation problem that forces the Fed to hold restrictive policy all year, but the available evidence says communications and market pricing had already shifted toward cuts as a plausible base case. I would make cuts more likely than not, but not near-certain because inflation remained above target and the Fed could prioritize credibility if the economy stayed hot.
attempts.strongest_yes: The Fed's own December 2023 projections implied multiple 2024 cuts if inflation kept cooling, and the starting policy rate was high enough that even modest additional disinflation or labor-market softening could make at least one cut appropriate during the year.
attempts.strongest_no: Inflation was still above the 2% target and labor markets were resilient, so a reacceleration in inflation or continued economic strength could lead the FOMC to maintain the 5.25% to 5.50% range throughout 2024 to protect anti-inflation credibility.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether inflation continues cooling toward target or stalls above 2%.","Whether resilient labor markets keep wage and demand pressure high enough to delay cuts.","How much evidence the FOMC requires before beginning an easing cycle.","Whether financial conditions loosen prematurely and complicate the inflation path.","Whether the Fed treats 2024 cuts as baseline normalization or waits for clearer economic weakness."]
attempts.premortem: This forecast resolves false if early-2024 inflation proves sticky or reaccelerates, labor markets remain strong, and the FOMC decides that cutting even once would risk undoing progress against inflation. In that world, December 2023 projections were conditional rather than a firm plan, and policymakers spend 2024 repeatedly delaying expected cuts until the year ends with the target range unchanged.

output.rationale: Averaged 3 differentiated forecaster probabilities. Review component attempts for disagreement and source quality.

attempts.rationale: Starting from the reference class in the evidence packet, the United States had won the last four men's Olympic basketball gold medals entering Paris 2024, implying a very strong recent Olympic base rate. The base-rate prior should therefore be well above 50%, because the US combines repeated Olympic success with the deepest global talent pool. I discount from that high base rate because Olympic basketball is not a season-long league: roster commitment, injuries, chemistry, and single-elimination variance matter, and recent FIBA tournaments showed that strong international teams can punish a US roster that is less than fully committed or poorly balanced. The evidence that early signals pointed toward a star-heavy US roster after the 2023 World Cup disappointment pushes the estimate back upward. Overall, the best base-rate estimate is that the US is the clear favorite but not close to certain.
